title: Lesson 303
transcript: Continuing our discussion of risk management, value, risks, treatment. And within treatment, we have avoid, transfer, mitigate, and accept. and within mitigation we've talked about the different types of the different controls we can have we can have directive deterrent preventative detective corrective recovery and compensating Let's now talk about how we can take these different controls and put them into different categories. So it's all those controls we talked about, but we can organize, categorize them in different ways. The first categorization we're gonna talk about is the difference between safeguards and countermeasures. Safeguards are controls that you put in place to try and prevent a risk. Because as much as possible, we always want to try and prevent a risk from occurring. So out of the controls that we've talked about, how do you try and prevent a risk from occurring? Well, those are your directive, deterrent, and especially your preventative controls. But unfortunately, we can never prevent every single risk from occurring. So that's where we need countermeasures because countermeasures are what we use to detect and respond to a risk recurring. And so What's the category that when you think about what controls fit within the category of countermeasures? Well, first of all, it's your detective controls because you have to detect if a risk has occurred so you can then do something. So if you've detected that a risk has occurred, what can you then do? Well, this is where you have your corrective recovery and possibly compensating controls. So that's the difference, safeguards, try and prevent a risk, countermeasures, detect and then deal with the risk as if it occurs. Another categorization that you've likely heard about is this idea of having administrative, technological and physical controls. And the idea here is that for all of the controls that we've talked about, you can slot and find examples of each of these controls under administrative, technological or physical. And so you can create a table like I'm showing you on your screen here. And so, for instance, an example of a directive administrative control would be a policy. An example of, say, preventative logical technical control would be a password. And the example of, say, physical recovery control would be rebuilding a building that's burned to the ground. So you could fill this entire table with examples of how each of these match up. Administrative detective controls would be reviewing documents, for instance. So you can fill this entire table, but you don't need to memorize that. What you do need to understand is what do these different categories mean? Administrative controls are the people and the process type things. So this is where Administrative controls are things like policies, business processes of reviewing things and creating documents, that sort of stuff. Those are all administrative controls. Technical logical controls are the technical stuff, like our firewalls, our security kernels that we're going to talk about later, our access control mechanisms, our IDS IPS systems. Any of these are technical logical controls. Now here's a big hint for the exam. We tend to lump technical and logical together as though they are the same thing. But there is a difference that sometimes is questioned on the exam. So what's the difference between technical and logical? Well, whenever we have a device, think of a firewall as an example. A firewall is essentially made up of two major components. You have the hardware and the software. The hardware is the technical. The software is the logical. And so if you get a question between the difference of what is technical and logical, technical is the hardware, logical is the software. And then the final category of controls is physical. And these are all of our physical controls, like signage, fences, hungry dogs, security cards asleep at their desk, camera systems, doors, windows, all of this, UPSs, generators. Those are our physical controls. Now, this next concept is really important. And it's this idea that every single control has two components to it. The first is functional. What does that mean? Well, every control that you implement is meant to do something. So what is an access control mechanism meant to do? Well, it's meant to control who can get access to the system. What's a firewall meant to do? Well, it's meant to control traffic between two network segments. This is the functional aspect of a control, whatever it's meant to do. But we also want to make sure that our controls are working correctly, that they're operating effectively. And that's the assurance piece. So for every control that we implement, it has to have a functional piece. It's got to do what it's supposed to do. But then we also want to make sure that it's actually working correctly, get assurance that it's working correctly on an ongoing basis. And we usually do this through logging and monitoring and that sort of thing. But the important part is whenever you implement any type of control, directive, deterrent, preventative, corrective, compensating, any of them, That control should have both a functional and an assurance aspect. Now, I have a fun way for you to remember this perhaps. So when John draws this, he always talks about how there's pillars. There's the functional pillar and there's the assurance pillar. But whenever he draws this, I'm making sure he's not paying attention right now. Whenever he draws this, he tends to draw the pillars as kind of like angled outwards, and whenever I see him drawing this, I don't know, my mind goes to a weird place, and I imagine that instead of being pillars, what they actually are are like tractor beams, and coming from an alien spaceship because he always draws the uh the controls this kind of like oval shape at the top and so uh here you go i've got this lovely slide that i've built for you which shows a uh a spaceship beaming up cows because that's what they beam up right so that's a way that you can think of functional and assurance it's um it's the control spaceship beaming up cows I don't know how that's gonna help you, but there you go.
